questions.question: Explain the difference between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation with examples.
keyPoints: Internal fragmentation: wasted space within allocated block due to fixed allocation unit size
External fragmentation: scattered free memory blocks that cannot satisfy large requests
Example of internal: allocating 16KB when process needs 15KB (1KB wasted)
Example of external: free holes of 2KB, 3KB, 1KB scattered between allocated blocks

questions.question: What is virtual memory? Explain how it enables execution of programs larger than physical memory.
keyPoints: Virtual memory extends physical memory using disk space
Uses paging or segmentation to map virtual addresses to physical addresses
Pages not currently needed are stored on disk (swap space)
When needed, pages are brought back to RAM (demand paging)
Provides illusion of larger contiguous memory to programs

questions.question: Describe the FIFO page replacement algorithm with an example showing how page faults occur.
keyPoints: FIFO (First In First Out) replaces the oldest page in memory
Maintains a queue of pages in memory order
When page fault occurs, remove page at front of queue
Example with 3 frames and page sequence: 1,2,3,4,1,2,5 shows how faults occur
Simple to implement but may not give optimal results

questions.question: What is paging? Discuss its advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Paging divides virtual and physical memory into fixed-size blocks (pages and frames)
Eliminates external fragmentation (advantage)
Enables non-contiguous memory allocation
May suffer from internal fragmentation (disadvantage)
Requires page table for address translation
Hardware support needed (MMU)

questions.question: Explain the concept of segmentation. How does it differ from paging?
keyPoints: Segmentation divides memory based on logical divisions (code, data, stack segments)
Each segment has variable size and distinct purpose
Provides natural protection between different program parts
Unlike paging: segments have semantic meaning, variable sizes
Paging: fixed-size uniform blocks, no logical meaning
Both can be combined (segmented paging)

questions.question: What is thrashing? Explain its causes and effects on system performance.
keyPoints: Thrashing: excessive paging activity where system spends more time swapping than executing
Causes: too many processes, insufficient physical memory, poor page replacement algorithm
Effects: severe degradation in CPU utilization, high I/O load, system becomes unresponsive
Solution: reduce degree of multiprogramming, increase memory, improve replacement algorithm
Working set model can help prevent thrashing

questions.question: Explain the LRU (Least Recently Used) page replacement algorithm and justify why it is better than FIFO.
keyPoints: LRU replaces the page that has not been used for the longest time
Based on principle of locality of reference: recently used pages likely to be used again
Does not suffer from Belady's anomaly (unlike FIFO)
Implementation can be done using counters or stack
Better performance in practice as it tracks actual usage not just arrival time

questions.question: Discuss various page replacement algorithms (FIFO, LRU, Optimal) with advantages, disadvantages, and comparative analysis.
keyPoints: FIFO: Simple, uses queue, suffers from Belady's anomaly, does not consider usage
LRU: Uses locality, better performance, but may be costly to implement
Optimal: Best theoretical performance, but requires future knowledge (unrealistic)
Comparison: In practice, LRU接近 Optimal performance but has overhead
Belady's anomaly: FIFO can have more faults with more frames
Implementation trade-offs: hardware support, overhead vs accuracy

questions.question: Explain demand paging and describe the steps involved in handling a page fault by the operating system.
keyPoints: Demand paging loads pages only when referenced (lazy loading)
Initial pages loaded on first reference, not all at start
Page fault handling steps: 1) Check page table, page not in memory
2) Locate required page in swap space or executable file
3) Find free frame in physical memory
4) Read page from disk into frame
5) Update page table with frame number
6) Restart the interrupted instruction
Role of MMU and TLB in address translation

questions.question: Compare contiguous memory allocation, paging, and segmentation memory management techniques.
keyPoints: Contiguous: simple, fast access, but suffers from external fragmentation, cannot run large programs
Paging: fixed-size blocks, no external fragmentation, internal fragmentation possible, simple address translation
Segmentation: logical divisions, variable size, provides protection, external fragmentation possible
Comparison table: fragmentation, memory utilization, access time, protection, complexity
Paging and segmentation can be combined for benefits of both
Choice depends on program structure and system requirements

questions.question: Explain the working of Memory Management Unit (MMU) in translating logical addresses to physical addresses in a paged memory system.
keyPoints: MMU sits between CPU and memory, performs address translation
Logical address divided into: page number and offset
Page number indexed into page table to get frame number
Frame number combined with offset to form physical address
TLB acts as cache for recent translations
Hardware can use inverted page tables for large systems
Protection bits checked during translation for access control
